nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing, highly oxygenated rocky river sections in the upper Orinoco and Casiquiare drainages; typically associated with granitic boulders and rock faces in rapids and riffles with warm, soft, slightly acidic to neutral water

decorPreferences: large smooth rounded boulders and rock stacks for grazing surfaces and territory
multiple snug caves (ceramic cave tubes, slate caves, or coconut shells) per specimen
driftwood for additional cover and biofilm grazing
strong directional water flow (powerhead or wavemaker) to replicate Orinoco rapids
fine sand or rounded gravel substrate
subdued lighting to reduce stress and encourage daytime activity

diet.notes: Unlike its close relative the Blue Phantom Pleco (L128), which tends toward carnivory, the Green Phantom is generally considered a competent biofilm and algae grazer. Diet should center on high-quality sinking algae and spirulina wafers and pellets, supplemented with blanched vegetables such as zucchini, cucumber, and squash, and periodic protein-rich frozen foods such as bloodworms, mysis shrimp, and daphnia. Driftwood in the tank provides supplemental fiber and supports the biofilm that the fish actively grazes.

goodWith: mid-water tetras and characins (cardinal, rummy-nose, lemon tetras)
peaceful dwarf cichlids sharing similar parameters (Apistogramma spp.)
Corydoras and other bottom-dwellers occupying different territories
other Loricariids when each has its own dedicated cave
avoidWith: other plecos of similar shape in confined tanks without multiple non-overlapping cave territories
Blue Phantom Pleco (L128) in small tanks — territorial conflict between related Hemiancistrus species
large aggressive cichlids that may outcompete at feeding time
fish requiring alkaline hard water (incompatible parameters)
very small nano fish that may be intimidated by adult specimens

summary: The Green Phantom Pleco (Hemiancistrus subviridis, L200) is a boldly patterned catfish from the rocky rapids of Venezuela's upper Orinoco and Casiquiare drainages. Unlike the related Blue Phantom (L128), it tends toward algae and biofilm grazing rather than carnivory. It requires warm, highly oxygenated water with strong flow and is generally considered intermediate to keep.
careNotes:
The Green Phantom Pleco was formally described as *Hemiancistrus subviridis* in 2005 (Werneke et al.); prior to that it circulated in the hobby under its L-number designation L200, assigned by hobbyist publications. The L200 designation is shared with a visually similar species, *Baryancistrus demantoides* (sometimes traded as 'L200 High-Fin' or 'L200a'), and the two are commonly confused at point of sale; the true *H. subviridis* lacks the elongated dorsal-fin lobe and tends to be the more uniformly olive-green form. Both fish are routinely sold under the L200 label because the trade rarely distinguishes between them reliably.

The species originates from the upper Orinoco and Casiquiare drainages in Venezuela (and likely Colombia), where it is generally found among granitic boulders in fast-flowing, highly oxygenated rapids. Captive setups should reflect this: warm water around 24–28°C (some sources extend up to 30°C; the lower end of that range is safer for long-term keeping), pH 6.5–7.5, soft to moderately hard, with strong directional flow from a powerhead or wavemaker and excellent surface agitation. Water quality must be high — Hemiancistrus species tend to be sensitive to nitrate accumulation and dissolved organic buildup; weekly partial water changes of 25–30% are commonly advised.

Unlike the Blue Phantom Pleco, the Green Phantom is generally considered a competent algae and biofilm grazer, though it will not eradicate a serious algae problem and should not be purchased solely for that purpose. A varied diet centered on algae/spirulina wafers, blanched vegetables, and occasional protein-rich frozen foods produces the best long-term condition.

The species is reported as fairly peaceful toward unrelated tankmates but territorial toward conspecifics and similarly-shaped Loricariids, particularly between adult males. A single specimen should be given at least two or three cave options; for multiple specimens, plan on at least 300–400 L with multiple non-overlapping cave territories. Captive-bred specimens, increasingly available from specialist breeders, are hardier than wild-caught and are preferred.
breedingNotes: Breeding has been reported by specialist keepers and is considered moderate to difficult. The Green Phantom is a cave spawner: the male selects and guards a cave, the female deposits an adhesive clutch (typically ~40 eggs), and the male fans and guards the eggs through hatching (commonly 3–5 days at 28°C). Fry remain in the cave for several days post-hatch and can begin accepting finely crushed sinking pellets, powdered algae food, and newly hatched brine shrimp roughly 10 days after hatching. Conditioning with live and frozen protein-rich foods plus cool partial water changes to simulate the onset of the rainy season are commonly used to trigger spawning. A dedicated breeding tank with soft, slightly acidic warm water, strong flow, and multiple cave options improves success rates.

primary.name: PlanetCatfish — Catfish of the Month: Hemiancistrus subviridis
primary.notes: Primary source per playbook (species_id=240 endpoint returned 403, fell back to the dedicated Catfish of the Month profile article for the species). Confirmed L200 designation, Orinoco/Casiquiare origin in Venezuela, rocky-rapids biotope, omnivorous algae-grazing diet, cave-spawning reproductive mode, and adult size ~15–20 cm.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Hemiancistrus subviridis
additional.notes: Taxonomic confirmation (Loricariidae, Siluriformes), 2005 formal description (Werneke et al.), native range (Orinoco and Casiquiare drainages, Venezuela), habitat (granitic rocks in flowing water), max SL 15 cm. Also confirmed L-200 designation is shared with Baryancistrus demantoides, which the trade routinely confuses with this species.

additional.name: Aquatic Community — Green Phantom Pleco L200
additional.notes: Secondary used in place of SeriouslyFish (which returned 404 for this species page). Confirmed water parameters (pH 6.0–8.0, temp 22–25°C), max size ~19 cm, peaceful temperament with mild territorial bickering among Loricariids, cave-spawning breeding with ~40 eggs and ~4-day hatch time, fast-flow rocky decor requirement.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net — Lemon-Spotted Green Pleco (H. subviridis)
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (24–28°C, pH 6.6–7.3, gH 6–21°N), adult size 14–18 cm, lifespan 8–12 years, minimum tank length 120 cm, biofilm-grazing omnivore diet, semi-aggressive toward conspecifics and similarly-shaped Loricariids while peaceful in community.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory — Green Phantom Pleco Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked broader temperature range (22–30°C), pH 6.5–7.5, hardness 5–15 dGH, adult size 18–20 cm, minimum tank volume ~190 L (50 US gal) for one specimen with larger needed for groups, lifespan 10–15 years, conspecific territoriality, and Orinoco native range.

additional.name: Aqua Imports — L200 Green Phantom Pleco
additional.notes: Specialty importer's product page. Recommends 75 US gal (~284 L) minimum, temp 78–82°F (~26–28°C), pH 6.5–7.5, omnivorous grazing diet with sinking prepared foods and fresh vegetables, moderate-to-strong current, plenty of hiding places.

fish.breedingNotes: The Green Phantom Pleco is a cave spawner. The male selects and guards a cave, and a typical clutch runs around 40 eggs, which hatch in roughly 3 to 5 days at approximately 28°C. Fry generally begin accepting food around 10 days after hatching. Conditioning with live and frozen foods combined with cool partial water changes to simulate the onset of the rainy season is commonly used to trigger spawning. Breeding has been reported by specialist keepers but is not considered routine.
